one_line_takeaway: Gzip-based compression ratio and related structural features reveal a length-dependent statistical regularity gap between human and LLM-generated/mediated text that grows in long-form controlled and encyclopedic settings but attenuates or reverses in short, fully synthetic social posts -- a descriptive characterization study, not a security or detection paper.
key_finding: Compression ratio consistently separates human from LLM/LLM-mediated text in the controlled and knowledge-mediation settings, with the gap growing as documents lengthen (LLM text becomes progressively more compressible after ~20 sentences while human text plateaus; Wikipedia vs. Grokipedia gap emerges after ~50 sentences then narrows again past ~500 sentences due to Grok's front-loaded rewriting). In the fully synthetic social setting (Moltbook vs. Reddit), the separation is much weaker and only holds over a short range of post lengths (attenuating after ~25 sentences), and interestingly LLM (Moltbook) content shows slightly LOWER compressibility (higher R) than human Reddit text in that regime -- the opposite direction from the other two settings -- which the authors attribute to conversational prompting inducing more casual, locally-varied style. Binary human-vs-LLM classification accuracy using only these compression/lexical features was 0.93 (macro F1 0.88) on the controlled corpus, 0.85 (macro F1 0.85) on Wikipedia/Grokipedia, and 0.88 (macro F1 0.88) on Reddit/Moltbook -- comparable to a cited prior detector (66% accuracy, 60 lexical/grammatical/rhetorical features) despite using a much narrower, purely structural feature set. Seven-way model-family classification (Human, GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, Llama 3 8B/70B base and Instruct) reached only 0.65 accuracy (macro F1 0.65), with base Llama variants most confusable (F1 0.46-0.66) and GPT/human classes most separable (F1 0.75-0.82); collapsing to Human/GPT/Llama family level raised accuracy to 0.93 (macro F1 0.90).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is a model-agnostic, compression-based (gzip) measure of the structural statistical regularity of text, used to characterize how probabilistic (LLM) generation reshapes language structure relative to human writing. Using the gzip compression ratio R(x)=C(x)/|x| and a family of derived features (conditional compression ratio, prefix-compression curves/slope, word-order shuffle gap and Normalized Compression Distance, normalized character/word entropy, type-token ratio, repetition-distance statistics), the authors show across three settings of increasing realism -- (1) a controlled human-vs-LLM continuation corpus, (2) Wikipedia vs. Grokipedia (LLM-mediated rewrites of encyclopedic pages), and (3) Reddit vs. Moltbook (fully LLM-agent-generated social posts) -- that LLM-generated text is generally more compressible (lower R) than human text, that this gap grows with document length (LLM compressibility increases after ~20 sentences while human compressibility stays flat), and that the separation attenuates in short/fragmented text (Moltbook posts, gap narrows after ~25 sentences). The authors explicitly state their goal is NOT to build a synthetic-text detector or evaluate semantic quality, but to identify a structural signature of probabilistic generation; the Histogram Gradient Boosting classifiers trained on these features are presented as an evaluation of the discriminative strength of the signal, not as the paper's deliverable.
threat_model: None. The paper is a computational-linguistics / information-theory study of LLM output statistics, not an attack, defense, or vulnerability-assessment paper. It has no adversary, no attacker capabilities, and no security objective; it explicitly disclaims being a synthetic-text detector. It is adjacent to the AI-text-detection literature (which itself has security/misinformation motivations, per Related Work) but the paper's own framing and contribution are purely descriptive/structural.
limitations: The authors themselves note: (1) beyond a length threshold in the Wikipedia/Grokipedia setting, the compression-based gap narrows because Grok's rewriting is concentrated at the start of pages, so longer prefixes dilute the LLM-modified fraction -- a confound tied to how Grokipedia specifically was produced, not a general property; (2) the fully synthetic social setting shows the signal is scale/length-dependent and can even reverse direction, implying no universal, length-independent compressibility signature exists; (3) the study explicitly does not claim the compression signal indicates anything about semantic quality, truthfulness, or evaluates real-world detector robustness (e.g., against paraphrasing, adversarial evasion, or human post-editing) -- those are left to future work; (4) gzip and the derived feature set are one specific, generic operationalization of 'structural regularity' and results may not generalize to other compressors or to LLM outputs produced under different decoding/sampling settings than those in the corpora studied.

datasets_used: Human-AI Parallel English Corpus (Reinhart et al.) -- human continuations vs. GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, Llama 3.1 8B, Llama 3.1 70B, and their Instruct variants
Wikipedia vs. Grokipedia paired pages (9,279 pages, high-reference-count/high-revision-activity subset)

Reddit (pre-2018 posts via Pushshift API) vs. Moltbook (LLM-agent social platform, data collected Jan 31-Feb 10, 2026), 10,000 posts per platform stratified into 4 length buckets, mid+high buckets used = 20,000 posts per platform
quantitative_results: Controlled corpus, 7-class model-identity classifier: accuracy 0.65, macro F1 0.65; GPT-family/human F1 0.75-0.82, base Llama models F1 0.46-0.66
Controlled corpus, 3-class family classifier (Human/GPT/Llama): accuracy 0.93, macro F1 0.90
Controlled corpus, binary Human vs LLM classifier: accuracy 0.93, macro F1 0.88; LLM F1 0.96, Human F1 0.80
Wikipedia vs Grokipedia binary classifier: accuracy 0.85, macro F1 0.85; Wikipedia recall 0.94/F1 0.86; Grokipedia precision 0.93/recall 0.76/F1 0.84
Reddit vs Moltbook binary classifier: accuracy 0.88, macro F1 0.88; both classes precision/recall/F1 approx 0.87-0.88
Human document length distribution peaks at 479 words; analysis restricted to 466-489 word range (1st-3rd quartile)

LLM-text compressibility increase becomes apparent after roughly 20 sentences (controlled corpus); Wikipedia/Grokipedia gap emerges around 50 sentences and narrows near 500 sentences; Moltbook/Reddit gap narrows after roughly 25 sentences
baselines_compared: Randomly generated documents via uniform word sampling and empirical-frequency-weighted word sampling from the corpus vocabulary (used as reference low-structure baselines, both less compressible than human or LLM text)

Prior detection work (Reinhart et al., cited as [35]) reporting 66% accuracy on 7-way model identification using 60 lexical/grammatical/rhetorical features, compared against this paper's 0.65 accuracy using only compression-derived features
